intro: C++ relies on native compilation and deterministic resource management via RAII, giving developers precise control over system memory layouts. Java routes source execution through a cross-platform Virtual Machine (JVM), relying on a dynamic garbage collector and bytecode verifier to ensure absolute memory runtime safety. Moving codebases from C++ to Java trades away manual memory layout controls to improve cross-platform uniformity, application safety, and enterprise ecosystem scale.

diffs.from: Static nominal typing supported by powerful compile-time templates, multi-inheritance, and structural pointer casts.
diffs.to: Static nominal system with single inheritance, interfaces, and runtime-erased generic type validation rules.

diffs.from: Verbose procedural and object-oriented syntax featuring explicit memory access tokens (`*`, `&`, `->`).
diffs.to: Strictly object-oriented, clean structural syntax where all operational contexts must reside within class blocks.

diffs.from: Manual and RAII-driven stack/heap tracking using constructors, destructors, and explicit allocator boundaries.
diffs.to: Automated runtime tracking governed by a highly optimized, configurable virtual machine garbage collector.

diffs.from: Direct hardware thread access, memory fencing mechanisms, and low-level atomic primitives.
diffs.to: Built-in thread objects, synchronized locks, robust concurrency libraries, and modern Virtual Threads (Project Loom).

diffs.from: The STL, which focuses heavily on high-speed computational algorithms and value-type vector layouts.
diffs.to: The extensive JDK, providing out-of-the-box enterprise networking tools, cross-platform UI systems, and unified database layers.

examples.title: Class and Inheritance Structuring
examples.description: Converting a C++ class with manual destructor rules into a standard garbage-collected Java class.

examples.title: Polymorphic Collection Management
examples.description: Replacing explicit pointer vector stores with a managed reference List collection.

tips.title: Replace Destructors with AutoCloseable Implementations
tips.body: Java lacks deterministic destructors. For unmanaged resources like file handles or sockets, implement `AutoCloseable` and wrap usage in `try-with-resources` statements.

tips.title: Understand Java Reference Semantics
tips.body: Object variables in Java are implicit references, not local value copies. Assigning one class variable to another copies the reference pointer, not the underlying object data.

tips.title: Watch Out for Generic Type Erasure
tips.body: Unlike C++ templates that compile unique binary variations for each type parameter, Java erases generic parameters at compile time, replacing them with generic `Object` bounds.

tips.title: Utilize Final Fields to Enforce Immutability
tips.body: Replace C++ `const` parameter modifications by using the `final` keyword to ensure variables or fields cannot be reassigned after their initial binding.
